participants.extracted: The qualitative study invited staff from the 11 hospitals that participated in Stroke 90:10. Interviewees were hospital staff who had been members of the Stroke 90:10 teams, spanning radiographers, stroke coordinators, specialist stroke nurses, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, healthcare assistants, data collection staff, emergency department staff, ward managers, and members of the hospital executive. In addition, a focus group was held with the QIC faculty team.

interventions.extracted: A quality improvement collaborative (Stroke 90:10) based on the Institute for Healthcare Improvement Breakthrough Series model was implemented to improve stroke care in 24 participating hospitals. The collaborative aimed for 90% compliance with nine evidence-based stroke care indicators grouped into two bundles: an early-hours bundle and a rehabilitation bundle. The intervention included three learning sessions, action periods between sessions in which teams used Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles to test and scale up local changes, and support from an expert faculty. Additional support included executive mentoring visits, access to a project director and improvement advisor, weekly online sharing and learning through an extranet, and expectation of chief executive commitment to release staff for collaborative activities. Hospitals participated in phases, with initial sites serving as the intervention group and later sites acting as controls during the first phase.

outcomes.extracted: The study planned to measure process and quality-of-care outcomes for the Stroke 90:10 quality improvement collaborative using the nine evidence-based stroke care indicators grouped into two bundles. The early-hours bundle included: brain imaging within 24 hours of hospital admission to confirm stroke type; delivery of aspirin or an alternative antiplatelet within 24 hours of admission when clinically indicated; swallow screen within 24 hours of admission; and weight assessment on admission. The rehabilitation bundle included: physiotherapy assessment within 72 hours of admission; occupational therapy assessment within four days of admission; mood assessment during the inpatient stay; documented evidence of multidisciplinary team goals set for rehabilitation; and an additional rehabilitation indicator listed in Table 1 (partially shown in the source). These outcomes were measured as adherence/compliance with each indicator, typically using monthly monitoring data collected throughout the collaborative period, with overall performance assessed over the 12-month phase of the collaborative and compared across participating hospitals and phases. The qualitative substudy also examined participant-perceived effects on stroke-care quality, collaboration, and organizational functioning through semi-structured interviews and a focus group, but these were exploratory and not formal trial outcomes.
